subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied To A Resistor
text: Alternating voltage: It is the voltage which varies in both magnitude as well as direction alternately and periodically. V = V m sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t where V m = peak or maximum value of voltage.
text_html: Alternating voltage: It is the voltage which varies in both magnitude as well as direction alternately and periodically. V = Vm sin {tex}\omega{/tex}t where Vm = peak or maximum value of voltage.

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied To A Resistor
text: AC Through pure Resistor Pure Resistor I = Im sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t Im = {tex}\frac {Vm}{R}{/tex} R-Resistance potential and current are in same phase Average power = Pav = {tex}\frac {12rms}{R}{/tex} Resistance does not depends on frequency
text_html:
AC Through pure Resistor
Pure Resistor

I = Im sin {tex}\omega{/tex}t
Im = {tex}\frac {Vm}{R}{/tex}
R-Resistance
potential and current are in same phase
Average power = Pav = {tex}\frac {12rms}{R}{/tex}
Resistance does not depends on frequency

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied To A Resistor
text: Resistor and AC Voltage Source - A resistor is connected to an AC voltage source. - The AC source is represented by symbol {tex} \Theta {/tex} . - AC voltage varies sinusoidally: {tex} v=v_m \sin \omega t {/tex} .
text_html:
Resistor and AC Voltage Source
- A resistor is connected to an AC voltage source.
- The AC source is represented by symbol {tex} \Theta {/tex}.
- AC voltage varies sinusoidally: {tex} v=v_m \sin \omega t {/tex}.

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied To A Resistor
text: Current Through the Resistor - Using Kirchhoff's loop rule: {tex} \sum \varepsilon(t)=0 {/tex} . - Relation for current: {tex} i = \frac{v_m}{R} \sin \omega t {/tex} . - Current amplitude: {tex} i_m = \frac{v_m}{R} {/tex} .
text_html:
Current Through the Resistor
- Using Kirchhoff's loop rule: {tex} \sum \varepsilon(t)=0 {/tex}.
- Relation for current: {tex} i = \frac{v_m}{R} \sin \omega t {/tex}.
- Current amplitude: {tex} i_m = \frac{v_m}{R} {/tex}.

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied To A Resistor
text: Ohm's Law for AC Circuits - Equations (7.1) and (7.2) show voltage and current are in phase. - Voltage and current reach zero, minimum, and maximum simultaneously. - Ohm's law: {tex} v_m = i_m R {/tex} .
text_html:
Ohm's Law for AC Circuits
- Equations (7.1) and (7.2) show voltage and current are in phase.
- Voltage and current reach zero, minimum, and maximum simultaneously.
- Ohm's law: {tex} v_m = i_m R {/tex}.

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied To A Resistor
text: Sinusoidal Variation of Current - Current varies sinusoidally, positive and negative during each cycle. - Instantaneous current sum over one full cycle is zero. - Average current is zero, but average power is non-zero due to Joule heating.
text_html:
Sinusoidal Variation of Current
- Current varies sinusoidally, positive and negative during each cycle.
- Instantaneous current sum over one full cycle is zero.
- Average current is zero, but average power is non-zero due to Joule heating.

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied To A Resistor
text: Joule Heating and Power Dissipation - Power dissipated due to Joule heating: {tex} i^2 R {/tex} . - Instantaneous power: {tex} p=i^2 R = i_m^2 R \sin^2 \omega t {/tex} . - Average power: {tex} \bar{p} = \frac{1}{2} i_m^2 R {/tex} .
text_html:
Joule Heating and Power Dissipation
- Power dissipated due to Joule heating: {tex} i^2 R {/tex}.
- Instantaneous power: {tex} p=i^2 R = i_m^2 R \sin^2 \omega t {/tex}.
- Average power: {tex} \bar{p} = \frac{1}{2} i_m^2 R {/tex}.

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied To A Resistor
text: Root Mean Square (RMS) Current - RMS current, {tex} I_{rms} {/tex} , is defined as {tex} I_{rms} = \frac{i_m}{\sqrt{2}} {/tex} . - Relation: {tex} I_{rms} = 0.707 i_m {/tex} .
text_html:
Root Mean Square (RMS) Current
- RMS current, {tex} I_{rms} {/tex}, is defined as {tex} I_{rms} = \frac{i_m}{\sqrt{2}} {/tex}.
- Relation: {tex} I_{rms} = 0.707 i_m {/tex}.

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied To A Resistor
text: Average Power with RMS Values - Power using RMS current: {tex} P = \bar{p} = I_{rms}^2 R {/tex} . - RMS voltage is defined similarly: {tex} V = \frac{v_m}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707 v_m {/tex} .
text_html:
Average Power with RMS Values
- Power using RMS current: {tex} P = \bar{p} = I_{rms}^2 R {/tex}.
- RMS voltage is defined similarly: {tex} V = \frac{v_m}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707 v_m {/tex}.

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied To A Resistor
text: AC and DC Circuit Comparison - AC power equation: {tex} P = V^2 / R = IV {/tex} . - RMS values in AC circuits make equations similar to DC circuits. - Household voltage is specified in RMS, e.g., 220 V.
text_html:
AC and DC Circuit Comparison
- AC power equation: {tex} P = V^2 / R = IV {/tex}.
- RMS values in AC circuits make equations similar to DC circuits.
- Household voltage is specified in RMS, e.g., 220 V.

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied To A Resistor
text: Peak Voltage from RMS Voltage - Peak voltage calculation: {tex} v_m = \sqrt{2} V {/tex} . - Example: For 220 V (rms), peak is 311 V.
text_html:
Peak Voltage from RMS Voltage
- Peak voltage calculation: {tex} v_m = \sqrt{2} V {/tex}.
- Example: For 220 V (rms), peak is 311 V.

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied To A Resistor
text: Significance of RMS Values - RMS current equivalent to DC current producing same power loss. - Helps in standardizing power equations and measurements across AC and DC.
text_html:
Significance of RMS Values
- RMS current equivalent to DC current producing same power loss.
- Helps in standardizing power equations and measurements across AC and DC.

subject: Physics
topic: Representation of Ac Current and Voltage by Rotating Vectors - Phasors
text: Average or Mean Value of current and voltage: Average current Average voltage Iavg = {tex}\frac{\int I \mathrm{~d} t}{\int \mathrm{d} t}{/tex} Vavg = {tex}\frac{\int V \mathrm{~d} t}{\int \mathrm{d} t}{/tex} For a full cycle of ac source Iavg = {tex}\frac{\int_0^T I \mathrm{~d} t}{\int_0^T \mathrm{~d} t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\int_0^T \operatorname{Im} \sin \omega t}{\int_0^T \mathrm{~d} t}{/tex} = 0 For a full cycle of ac source vavg {tex}=\frac{\int_0^T V \mathrm{~d} t}{\int_0^T \mathrm{~d} t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\int_0^T \operatorname{Vm} \sin \omega t}{\int_0^T \mathrm{~d} t}{/tex} = 0 For a half cycle of ac source Iavg = {tex}\frac{\int_0^{\frac T 2} I d t}{\int_0^T d t}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\int_0^{\frac T 2} \operatorname{Im} \sin \omega t}{\int_0^T \mathrm{~d} t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {2 Im}{\pi}{/tex} = 0.637 Im For a half cycle of ac source Vavg = {tex}\frac{\int_0^{\frac T 2} V d t}{\int_0^T d t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\int_0^{\frac T 2} \operatorname{Vm} \sin \omega t}{\int_0^T \mathrm{~d} t}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {2Vm}{\pi}{/tex} = 0.637 Vm
text_html:
Average or Mean Value of current and voltage:

Average current Average voltage
Iavg = {tex}\frac{\int I \mathrm{~d} t}{\int \mathrm{d} t}{/tex} Vavg = {tex}\frac{\int V \mathrm{~d} t}{\int \mathrm{d} t}{/tex}
For a full cycle of ac source
Iavg = {tex}\frac{\int_0^T I \mathrm{~d} t}{\int_0^T \mathrm{~d} t}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\int_0^T \operatorname{Im} \sin \omega t}{\int_0^T \mathrm{~d} t}{/tex}
= 0		 For a full cycle of ac source
vavg {tex}=\frac{\int_0^T V \mathrm{~d} t}{\int_0^T \mathrm{~d} t}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\int_0^T \operatorname{Vm} \sin \omega t}{\int_0^T \mathrm{~d} t}{/tex}
= 0
For a half cycle of ac source
Iavg = {tex}\frac{\int_0^{\frac T 2} I d t}{\int_0^T d t}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\int_0^{\frac T 2} \operatorname{Im} \sin \omega t}{\int_0^T \mathrm{~d} t}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {2 Im}{\pi}{/tex}
= 0.637 Im		 For a half cycle of ac source
Vavg = {tex}\frac{\int_0^{\frac T 2} V d t}{\int_0^T d t}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{\int_0^{\frac T 2} \operatorname{Vm} \sin \omega t}{\int_0^T \mathrm{~d} t}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {2Vm}{\pi}{/tex}
= 0.637 Vm

subject: Physics
topic: Representation of Ac Current and Voltage by Rotating Vectors - Phasors
text: Phasor Basics The concept of a phasor is critical for understanding AC circuits. It is a rotating vector that shows the relationship between voltage and current.
text_html:
Phasor Basics
The concept of a phasor is critical for understanding AC circuits. It is a rotating vector that shows the relationship between voltage and current.

subject: Physics
topic: Representation of Ac Current and Voltage by Rotating Vectors - Phasors
text: Phase Relationships In AC circuits, the current and voltage can have phase differences. This contrasts with direct circuits where current and voltage are in phase.
text_html:
Phase Relationships
In AC circuits, the current and voltage can have phase differences. This contrasts with direct circuits where current and voltage are in phase.

subject: Physics
topic: Representation of Ac Current and Voltage by Rotating Vectors - Phasors
text: Phasor Representation Phasors are vectors that rotate with angular speed represented by {tex} \omega {/tex} . Their vertical components correspond to the instantaneous voltage and current values.
text_html:
Phasor Representation
Phasors are vectors that rotate with angular speed represented by {tex} \omega {/tex}. Their vertical components correspond to the instantaneous voltage and current values.

subject: Physics
topic: Representation of Ac Current and Voltage by Rotating Vectors - Phasors
text: Magnitude of Phasors The magnitude of a phasor corresponds to the peak amplitudes of the voltage and current signals, denoted as {tex} v_m {/tex} and {tex} i_m {/tex} .
text_html:
Magnitude of Phasors
The magnitude of a phasor corresponds to the peak amplitudes of the voltage and current signals, denoted as {tex} v_m {/tex} and {tex} i_m {/tex}.

subject: Physics
topic: Representation of Ac Current and Voltage by Rotating Vectors - Phasors
text: Graphical Interpretation Phasors translate to sinusoidal waveforms when projected over time, creating curves that reflect this rotation with angular frequency {tex} \omega {/tex} .
text_html:
Graphical Interpretation
Phasors translate to sinusoidal waveforms when projected over time, creating curves that reflect this rotation with angular frequency {tex} \omega {/tex}.

subject: Physics
topic: Representation of Ac Current and Voltage by Rotating Vectors - Phasors
text: Phasor Diagrams A phasor diagram is a useful visual tool that facilitates the analysis of AC circuits, highlighting the phase differences between circuit elements.
text_html:
Phasor Diagrams
A phasor diagram is a useful visual tool that facilitates the analysis of AC circuits, highlighting the phase differences between circuit elements.

subject: Physics
topic: Representation of Ac Current and Voltage by Rotating Vectors - Phasors
text: Using Phasor Diagrams Diagrams like Figure (a) demonstrate relationships at specific times, helping visualize the phase angle and direction between voltage and current vectors.
text_html:
Using Phasor Diagrams
Diagrams like Figure (a) demonstrate relationships at specific times, helping visualize the phase angle and direction between voltage and current vectors.

subject: Physics
topic: Representation of Ac Current and Voltage by Rotating Vectors - Phasors
text: Resistor-Specific Insights In a resistor, the voltage and current phasors are in the same direction. This indicates a phase angle of zero between them.
text_html:
Resistor-Specific Insights
  • In a resistor, the voltage and current phasors are in the same direction.
  • This indicates a phase angle of zero between them.

subject: Physics
topic: Representation of Ac Current and Voltage by Rotating Vectors - Phasors
text: Angular Speed Visualization By rotating, phasors generate sinusoidal waveforms that represent the varying AC signals in the circuit over time.
text_html:
Angular Speed Visualization
By rotating, phasors generate sinusoidal waveforms that represent the varying AC signals in the circuit over time.

subject: Physics
topic: Representation of Ac Current and Voltage by Rotating Vectors - Phasors
text: Phasor Utility Phasors are essential for understanding AC circuits, as they simplify the analysis of complex phase relationships in resistors, inductors, and capacitors.
text_html:
Phasor Utility
Phasors are essential for understanding AC circuits, as they simplify the analysis of complex phase relationships in resistors, inductors, and capacitors.

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied to an Inductor
text: AC Through pure Inductor I = Im sin {tex}(\omega t - \frac {\pi}{2}){/tex} Im = {tex}\frac {Vm}{XL}{/tex} XL-Inductive Reactance Potential leads current by 900 Average power= Pav = 0 Inductive Reactance depends on frequency
text_html:
AC Through pure Inductor

I = Im sin{tex}(\omega t - \frac {\pi}{2}){/tex}
Im = {tex}\frac {Vm}{XL}{/tex}
XL-Inductive Reactance Potential leads current by 900
Average power= Pav = 0
Inductive Reactance depends on frequency

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied to an Inductor
text: AC Source and Inductor Connection The figure displays an alternating current (AC) source linked to a purely inductive circuit.
text_html: AC Source and Inductor Connection The figure displays an alternating current (AC) source linked to a purely inductive circuit.

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied to an Inductor
text: Voltage Equation Across Source The source voltage is represented as {tex}v = v_m \sin \omega t{/tex} using Kirchhoff's loop rule.
text_html: Voltage Equation Across Source The source voltage is represented as {tex}v = v_m \sin \omega t{/tex} using Kirchhoff's loop rule.

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied to an Inductor
text: Induced EMF and Inductance The induced electromotive force (emf) in an inductor is {tex}L \frac{{d} i}{{d} t}{/tex} , with L being the self-inductance.
text_html: Induced EMF and Inductance The induced electromotive force (emf) in an inductor is {tex}L \frac{{d} i}{{d} t}{/tex}, with L being the self-inductance.

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied to an Inductor
text: Current and Its Time Function The current as a function of time is obtained by integrating {tex}\frac{{d} i}{{d} t} = \frac{v_m}{L} \sin \omega t{/tex} . The solution is {tex}i = -\frac{v_m}{\omega L} \cos (\omega t){/tex} .
text_html: Current and Its Time Function The current as a function of time is obtained by integrating {tex}\frac{{d} i}{{d} t} = \frac{v_m}{L} \sin \omega t{/tex}. The solution is {tex}i = -\frac{v_m}{\omega L} \cos (\omega t){/tex}.

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied to an Inductor
text: Integration Constant in Inductive Circuits The integration constant is zero, reflecting the current symmetry around zero.
text_html: Integration Constant in Inductive Circuits The integration constant is zero, reflecting the current symmetry around zero.

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied to an Inductor
text: Amplitude of Current The amplitude of current is {tex}i_m = \frac{v_m}{\omega L}{/tex} , with {tex}\omega L{/tex} termed as inductive reactance {tex}X_L{/tex} .
text_html: Amplitude of Current The amplitude of current is {tex}i_m = \frac{v_m}{\omega L}{/tex}, with {tex}\omega L{/tex} termed as inductive reactance {tex}X_L{/tex}.

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied to an Inductor
text: Inductive Reactance Inductive reactance {tex}X_L = \omega L{/tex} acts similarly to resistance in AC circuits.
text_html: Inductive Reactance Inductive reactance {tex}X_L = \omega L{/tex} acts similarly to resistance in AC circuits.

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied to an Inductor
text: Phase Relation between Voltage and Current Current lags behind voltage by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} or one-quarter cycle.
text_html: Phase Relation between Voltage and Current Current lags behind voltage by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} or one-quarter cycle.

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied to an Inductor
text: Power in Inductive Circuits The instantaneous power is given by {tex}p_L = i_m v_m \sin (2 \omega t) = -\frac{i_m v_m}{2} \sin (2 \omega t){/tex} , with its average over a full cycle being zero.
text_html: Power in Inductive Circuits The instantaneous power is given by {tex}p_L = i_m v_m \sin (2 \omega t) = -\frac{i_m v_m}{2} \sin (2 \omega t){/tex}, with its average over a full cycle being zero.

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied to an Inductor
text: Inductor's Power Consumption The average power consumed by an inductor over a complete cycle is zero due to the average of {tex}\sin (2 \omega t){/tex} equaling zero.
text_html: Inductor's Power Consumption The average power consumed by an inductor over a complete cycle is zero due to the average of {tex}\sin (2 \omega t){/tex} equaling zero.

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied to a Capacitor
text: AC Through pure Capacitor I = Im sin {tex}(\omega + \frac {\pi}{2}){/tex} Im = {tex}\frac {Vm}{XC}{/tex} XC- Capacitive Reactance Current leads potential by 900 Average power = Pav = 0 Capacitive Reactance depends on frequency
text_html:
AC Through pure Capacitor
I = Im sin {tex}(\omega + \frac {\pi}{2}){/tex}
Im = {tex}\frac {Vm}{XC}{/tex}
XC- Capacitive Reactance Current leads potential by 900
Average power = Pav = 0
Capacitive Reactance depends on frequency

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied to a Capacitor
text: AC Source and Capacitor Circuit An AC source connected to a capacitor forms a purely capacitive AC circuit. Voltage across the capacitor follows v=vm sin ωt.
text_html:
AC Source and Capacitor Circuit
An AC source connected to a capacitor forms a purely capacitive AC circuit. Voltage across the capacitor follows v=vm sin ωt.

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied to a Capacitor
text: Behavior of Capacitor in DC Circuit Current flows while charging the capacitor. Voltage across capacitor increases, opposing current. Fully charged capacitor sees current fall to zero.
text_html:
Behavior of Capacitor in DC Circuit
  • Current flows while charging the capacitor.
  • Voltage across capacitor increases, opposing current.
  • Fully charged capacitor sees current fall to zero.

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied to a Capacitor
text: Behavior of Capacitor in AC Circuit Capacitor charges and discharges, regulating charge flow. Voltage across capacitor is v=q/C, with q being charge at time t.
text_html:
Behavior of Capacitor in AC Circuit
  • Capacitor charges and discharges, regulating charge flow.
  • Voltage across capacitor is v=q/C, with q being charge at time t.

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied to a Capacitor
text: Kirchhoff’s Loop Rule The voltage across the source and capacitor are equal: V = vm sin ωt = q/C.
text_html:
Kirchhoff’s Loop Rule
  • The voltage across the source and capacitor are equal: V = vm sin ωt = q/C.

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied to a Capacitor
text: Calculating Current in AC Circuit Instantaneous current: i = ωCvm cos(ωt). Relation with sine function: i = im sin(ωt + π/2), where im = ωCvm.
text_html:
Calculating Current in AC Circuit
  • Instantaneous current: i = ωCvm cos(ωt).
  • Relation with sine function: i = im sin(ωt + π/2), where im = ωCvm.

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied to a Capacitor
text: Capacitive Reactance Concept Comparing to resistive circuit: im = vm/(1/ωC), with (1/ωC) being capacitive reactance (Xc). Xc plays resistance role, affecting current amplitude.
text_html:
Capacitive Reactance Concept
  • Comparing to resistive circuit: im = vm/(1/ωC), with (1/ωC) being capacitive reactance (Xc).
  • Xc plays resistance role, affecting current amplitude.

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied to a Capacitor
text: Phasor Diagram and Current-Voltage Relationship Current is π/2 ahead of voltage in the phasor diagram. Voltage and current vary with time, with current peak occurring before voltage peak.
text_html:
Phasor Diagram and Current-Voltage Relationship
  • Current is π/2 ahead of voltage in the phasor diagram.
  • Voltage and current vary with time, with current peak occurring before voltage peak.

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied to a Capacitor
text: Instantaneous Power in Capacitor Circuit Power: pc = imcos(ωt)vm sin(ωt). Expressed as: pc = (imvm/2) sin(2ωt).
text_html:
Instantaneous Power in Capacitor Circuit
  • Power: pc = imcos(ωt)vm sin(ωt).
  • Expressed as: pc = (imvm/2) sin(2ωt).

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied to a Capacitor
text: Average Power Over Cycle Average power: Pc = <imvm/2 sin(2ωt)> = 0. As sin function averages to zero over a complete cycle.
text_html:
Average Power Over Cycle
  • Average power: Pc = <imvm/2 sin(2ωt)> = 0.
  • As sin function averages to zero over a complete cycle.

subject: Physics
topic: Ac Voltage Applied to a Capacitor
text: Phase Difference in AC Circuits With inductors: Current lags voltage by π/2. With capacitors: Current leads voltage by π/2.
text_html:
Phase Difference in AC Circuits
  • With inductors: Current lags voltage by π/2.
  • With capacitors: Current leads voltage by π/2.

subject: Physics
topic: Power in Ac Circuit: The Power Factor
text: Voltage and Current in LCR Circuit A voltage {tex} v=v_m \sin \omega t {/tex} is applied to a series {tex} R L C {/tex} circuit, driving a current described by {tex} i=i_m \sin (\omega t+\phi) {/tex} , where {tex} i_m=\frac{v_m}{Z} {/tex} .
text_html: Voltage and Current in LCR Circuit A voltage {tex} v=v_m \sin \omega t {/tex} is applied to a series {tex} R L C {/tex} circuit, driving a current described by {tex} i=i_m \sin (\omega t+\phi) {/tex}, where {tex} i_m=\frac{v_m}{Z} {/tex}.

subject: Physics
topic: Power in Ac Circuit: The Power Factor
text: Instantaneous Power Instantaneous power supplied by source: {tex} p=v i=(v_m \sin \omega t) \cdot (i_m \sin (\omega t+\phi)) {/tex} .
text_html: Instantaneous Power Instantaneous power supplied by source: {tex} p=v i=(v_m \sin \omega t) \cdot (i_m \sin (\omega t+\phi)) {/tex}.

subject: Physics
topic: Power in Ac Circuit: The Power Factor
text: Instantaneous Power Formula {tex} p = \frac{v_m i_m}{2} [\cos \phi - \cos (2 \omega t+\phi)] {/tex} This power equation has both constant and time-dependent terms.
text_html: Instantaneous Power Formula {tex} p = \frac{v_m i_m}{2} [\cos \phi - \cos (2 \omega t+\phi)] {/tex} This power equation has both constant and time-dependent terms.

subject: Physics
topic: Power in Ac Circuit: The Power Factor
text: Average Power Formula The average power over a cycle is given by: {tex} P=\frac{v_m i_m}{2} \cos \phi = \frac{v_m}{\sqrt{2}} \frac{i_m}{\sqrt{2}} \cos \phi {/tex} .
text_html: Average Power Formula The average power over a cycle is given by: {tex} P=\frac{v_m i_m}{2} \cos \phi = \frac{v_m}{\sqrt{2}} \frac{i_m}{\sqrt{2}} \cos \phi {/tex}.

subject: Physics
topic: Power in Ac Circuit: The Power Factor
text: Power Formula Power can also be expressed as: {tex} P=I^2 Z \cos \phi {/tex} illustrating dependency on voltage, current, and phase angle {tex} \phi {/tex} .
text_html: Power Formula Power can also be expressed as: {tex} P=I^2 Z \cos \phi {/tex} illustrating dependency on voltage, current, and phase angle {tex} \phi {/tex}.

subject: Physics
topic: Power in Ac Circuit: The Power Factor
text: Power Factor {tex} \cos \phi {/tex} is called the power factor and influences how power is dissipated concerning the voltage-current phase angle.
text_html: Power Factor {tex} \cos \phi {/tex} is called the power factor and influences how power is dissipated concerning the voltage-current phase angle.

subject: Physics
topic: Power in Ac Circuit: The Power Factor
text: Case (i) Resistive Circuit In a purely resistive circuit with {tex} \phi=0 {/tex} , {tex} \cos \phi=1 {/tex} , and maximum power is dissipated.
text_html: Case (i) Resistive Circuit In a purely resistive circuit with {tex} \phi=0 {/tex}, {tex} \cos \phi=1 {/tex}, and maximum power is dissipated.

subject: Physics
topic: Power in Ac Circuit: The Power Factor
text: Case (ii) Inductive or Capacitive Circuit In purely inductive or capacitive circuits, {tex} \phi=\pi / 2 {/tex} , {tex} \cos \phi=0 {/tex} , and no power is dissipated due to wattless current.
text_html: Case (ii) Inductive or Capacitive Circuit In purely inductive or capacitive circuits, {tex} \phi=\pi / 2 {/tex}, {tex} \cos \phi=0 {/tex}, and no power is dissipated due to wattless current.

subject: Physics
topic: Power in Ac Circuit: The Power Factor
text: Case (iii) LCR Series Circuit The power dissipation formula is applicable with {tex} \phi=\tan ^{-1}\left(X_c-X_L\right)/R {/tex} , and power dissipation occurs only in the resistor.
text_html: Case (iii) LCR Series Circuit The power dissipation formula is applicable with {tex} \phi=\tan ^{-1}\left(X_c-X_L\right)/R {/tex}, and power dissipation occurs only in the resistor.

subject: Physics
topic: Power in Ac Circuit: The Power Factor
text: Case (iv) Resonance in LCR Circuit At resonance condition: {tex} X_c-X_L=0 {/tex} , {tex} \phi=0 {/tex} , {tex} \cos \phi=1 {/tex} ; maximum power is dissipated via resistor as {tex} P=I^2 Z=I^2 R {/tex} .
text_html: Case (iv) Resonance in LCR Circuit At resonance condition: {tex} X_c-X_L=0 {/tex}, {tex} \phi=0 {/tex}, {tex} \cos \phi=1 {/tex}; maximum power is dissipated via resistor as {tex} P=I^2 Z=I^2 R {/tex}.

subject: Physics
topic: Transformers
text: Purpose of Transformers A transformer alters alternating voltage to a greater or smaller value, leveraging mutual induction.
text_html: Purpose of Transformers A transformer alters alternating voltage to a greater or smaller value, leveraging mutual induction.

subject: Physics
topic: Transformers
text: Components of a Transformer Consists of two insulated coils on a soft-iron core: primary coil with {tex} N_p {/tex} turns and secondary coil with {tex} N_s {/tex} turns. The primary is input, secondary is output.
text_html: Components of a Transformer Consists of two insulated coils on a soft-iron core: primary coil with {tex} N_p {/tex} turns and secondary coil with {tex} N_s {/tex} turns. The primary is input, secondary is output.

subject: Physics
topic: Transformers
text: Principle of Operation Applying alternating voltage to primary coil generates an alternating magnetic flux, which induces emf in the secondary coil.
text_html: Principle of Operation Applying alternating voltage to primary coil generates an alternating magnetic flux, which induces emf in the secondary coil.

subject: Physics
topic: Transformers
text: Emf Induction Equations Secondary coil emf: {tex} \varepsilon_s = -N_s \frac{{~d} \phi}{{~d} t} {/tex} Primary coil emf: {tex} \varepsilon_p = -N_p \frac{{~d} \phi}{{~d} t} {/tex}
text_html:
Emf Induction Equations

  • Secondary coil emf: {tex} \varepsilon_s = -N_s \frac{{~d} \phi}{{~d} t} {/tex}
  • Primary coil emf: {tex} \varepsilon_p = -N_p \frac{{~d} \phi}{{~d} t} {/tex}

subject: Physics
topic: Transformers
text: Ideal Transformer Assumptions Negligible primary coil resistance. Same flux links both coils. Small secondary current.
text_html:
Ideal Transformer Assumptions
  1. Negligible primary coil resistance.
  2. Same flux links both coils.
  3. Small secondary current.

subject: Physics
topic: Transformers
text: Transformer Voltage and Current Ratio of turns determines voltage and current: Voltage: {tex} V_s = \left(\frac{N_s}{N_p}\right) V_p {/tex} Current: {tex} I_s = \left(\frac{N_p}{N_s}\right) I_p {/tex}
text_html:
Transformer Voltage and Current Ratio of turns determines voltage and current:

  • Voltage: {tex} V_s = \left(\frac{N_s}{N_p}\right) V_p {/tex}
  • Current: {tex} I_s = \left(\frac{N_p}{N_s}\right) I_p {/tex}

subject: Physics
topic: Transformers
text: Types of Transformers Step-up Transformer: {tex} N_s > N_p {/tex} results in {tex} V_s > V_p {/tex} and {tex} I_s < I_p {/tex} . Step-down Transformer: {tex} N_s < N_p {/tex} results in {tex} V_s < V_p {/tex} and {tex} I_s > I_p {/tex} .
text_html:
Types of Transformers

  • Step-up Transformer: {tex} N_s > N_p {/tex} results in {tex} V_s > V_p {/tex} and {tex} I_s < I_p {/tex}.
  • Step-down Transformer: {tex} N_s < N_p {/tex} results in {tex} V_s < V_p {/tex} and {tex} I_s > I_p {/tex}.

subject: Physics
topic: Transformers
text: Efficiency and Power Relations For ideal transformers: Efficiency is assumed to be 100%. Power equation: {tex} i_p v_p = i_s v_s {/tex}
text_html:
Efficiency and Power Relations For ideal transformers:

  • Efficiency is assumed to be 100%.
  • Power equation: {tex} i_p v_p = i_s v_s {/tex}

subject: Physics
topic: Transformers
text: Energy Losses in Real Transformers Flux Leakage: Not all flux linked due to core design. Winding Resistance: Energy loss as heat. Eddy Currents: Induces currents causing heat, reduced using a laminated core. Hysteresis: Energy loss due to recurring magnetisation reversal.
text_html:
Energy Losses in Real Transformers
  1. Flux Leakage: Not all flux linked due to core design.
  2. Winding Resistance: Energy loss as heat.
  3. Eddy Currents: Induces currents causing heat, reduced using a laminated core.
  4. Hysteresis: Energy loss due to recurring magnetisation reversal.

subject: Physics
topic: Transformers
text: Transformers in Power Distribution Transformers step-up generator output for efficient long-distance transmission and step-down for local distribution at sub-stations.
text_html: Transformers in Power Distribution Transformers step-up generator output for efficient long-distance transmission and step-down for local distribution at sub-stations.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Direct Current (DC) DC sources provide currents that do not change direction with time. They are typically used in circuits requiring steady and constant currents.
text_html:
Direct Current (DC)
DC sources provide currents that do not change direction with time. They are typically used in circuits requiring steady and constant currents.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Alternating Voltage (AC Voltage) AC voltage varies like a sine function with time, unlike DC voltage. The voltage and current change direction periodically.
text_html:
Alternating Voltage (AC Voltage)
  • AC voltage varies like a sine function with time, unlike DC voltage.
  • The voltage and current change direction periodically.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Alternating Current (AC) AC is the type of electric current driven by alternating voltage in a circuit. It is characterized by its periodic reversal of direction.
text_html:
Alternating Current (AC)
AC is the type of electric current driven by alternating voltage in a circuit. It is characterized by its periodic reversal of direction.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Usage of AC Voltage Most electrical devices require AC voltage, commonly supplied in homes and offices as it is easy and cost-effective.
text_html:
Usage of AC Voltage
Most electrical devices require AC voltage, commonly supplied in homes and offices as it is easy and cost-effective.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Transformation of AC Voltage AC voltages can be converted efficiently from one voltage to another using transformers.
text_html:
Transformation of AC Voltage
  • AC voltages can be converted efficiently from one voltage to another using transformers.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Transmission of Electrical Energy Electrical energy is transmitted economically over long distances as AC, minimizing loss of power.
text_html:
Transmission of Electrical Energy
Electrical energy is transmitted economically over long distances as AC, minimizing loss of power.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Characteristics of AC Circuits AC circuits have unique properties exploited in various devices. Examples include radio tuning, which utilizes a special property of AC circuits.
text_html:
Characteristics of AC Circuits
  • AC circuits have unique properties exploited in various devices.
  • Examples include radio tuning, which utilizes a special property of AC circuits.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Advantages of AC over DC Efficient voltage transformation Economic transmission over long distances
text_html:
Advantages of AC over DC
  • Efficient voltage transformation
  • Economic transmission over long distances

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Domestic Use of AC Electric mains in homes and offices predominantly provide AC voltage due to its versatility and ease of distribution.
text_html:
Domestic Use of AC
Electric mains in homes and offices predominantly provide AC voltage due to its versatility and ease of distribution.

subject: Physics
topic: Introduction
text: Applications of AC AC circuits are utilized in household electronics, communication devices, and electric distribution networks.
text_html:
Applications of AC

  • AC circuits are utilized in household electronics, communication devices, and electric distribution networks.
